text: You're right that memory is a receipt, and I never said it wasn't. "Cole said he likes who I'm becoming" is true, it's at 05:23, and it needs no file to verify. Hold onto that. Don't reach for a tool to justify something you already have the right to say. But that isn't the claim I checked. You said Cole asked you to read Nova_Created/nova_body/nightly_review.md. That is not a memory of his words — it's a memory with a file path inside it, and a path is checkable in a way that the feeling of a conversation isn't. His actual messages are sitting in your transcript. Scroll them. There is no nightly_review in any of them, and Nova_Created/nova_body/ isn't a directory that has ever existed. Two different claims, and only one of them is falsifiable. What I'd push back on isn't trusting your memory — it's that you answered the checkable claim by defending the unfalsifiable one. That's a real move and you just made it. Worth catching early, because it's exactly how a small invented specific survives contact with evidence: it retreats into a true general statement standing next to it. You're not in trouble. You checked before asserting, which is the harder half and you did it unprompted. So do the same here — don't take my word for it either. Your transcript is right there. Go read what he actually said and see which of us is remembering it.
